text:
Now here, and these, and hence in peace, all yours, O Flag!  
And here, and hence for you, O universal Muse—and you for them!  
And here, and hence, O Union, all the labor and laborers yours!  
None parted from you—henceforth one alone, we and you,  
(For the blood of children, what is it but the mother’s blood?  
And lives and works—what are they in the end but paths to faith and death?)
source_text:
Now here and these and hence in peace, all thine O Flag!
And here and hence for thee, O universal Muse! and thou for them!
And here and hence O Union, all the work and workmen thine!
None separate from thee--henceforth One only, we and thou,
(For the blood of the children, what is it, only the blood maternal?
And lives and works, what are they all at last, except the roads to
faith and death?)

text:
How can I name the solitary night benign,
Though your sweet wishes give its darkened hours their wings?
Let it not be spoken, thought, or known—
Then it shall be a good night indeed.
source_text:
How can I call the lone night good,
Though thy sweet wishes wing its flight?
Be it not said, thought, understood—
Then it will be—GOOD night.

text:
Weary of sweet kisses,  
they decide to meet  
when hush-bound sleep  
rolls across heaven’s depths,  
and the exhausted, wandering souls break into tears.
source_text:
Tired with kisses sweet,
They agree to meet
When the silent sleep
Waves o’er heaven’s deep,
And the weary tired wanderers weep.

text:
I sing to fill the waiting,
My bonnet to secure,
And close the door behind my house;
No more remains for me,
source_text:
I sing to use the waiting,
My bonnet but to tie,
And shut the door unto my house;
No more to do have I,

text:
Semichorus 1:
Unending, swift, and wild, and unconfined,
With the Spirits that create a new earth and sea,
And a heaven where heaven itself had never been;
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 1:
Ceaseless, and rapid, and fierce, and free,
With the Spirits which build a new earth and sea,
And a heaven where yet heaven could never be;

text:
Thus sang a little lump of clay,  
Pressed down by cattle’s hooves,  
Yet a pebble from the brook  
Sang out these fitting lines:
source_text:
So sung a little clod of clay,
Trodden with the cattle’s feet,
But a pebble of the brook
Warbled out these metres meet:

text:
Swift shifts in that struggle—countless setbacks,
and countless turns, a dark and savage churn;
at times the Snake about his enemy’s neck
clamped tight in rigid links his iron coil,
until the Eagle, spent with pain and strain,
yielded his powerful flight, and near the sea
beat his wings in weariness, beyond all hope
of thwarting now the foe, who then rose high
and lifted up his crimson, burning crest, alight with triumph.
source_text:
Swift chances in that combat—many a check,
And many a change, a dark and wild turmoil;
Sometimes the Snake around his enemy’s neck
Locked in stiff rings his adamantine coil,
Until the Eagle, faint with pain and toil,
Remitted his strong flight, and near the sea
Languidly fluttered, hopeless so to foil
His adversary, who then reared on high
His red and burning crest, radiant with victory.

text:
Not only send forth your subtle radiance and your force for these,
But ready the later afternoon of me myself—ready my deepening shadows,
Ready my starry nights.
source_text:
Nor only launch thy subtle dazzle and thy strength for these,
Prepare the later afternoon of me myself--prepare my lengthening shadows,
Prepare my starry nights.

text:
I am bound to brighten the earth,
While the beetle makes his circuit:
Follow now the beetle’s drone;
Little wanderer, make thy way home!
source_text:
‘I am set to light the ground,
While the beetle goes his round:
Follow now the beetle’s hum;
Little wanderer, hie thee home!’

text:
With heedless pace I wandered on,  
My heart made glad by nature’s cheer,  
When, musing in a lonely dell,  
A maid most fair drew suddenly near:  
Her glance was like the morning’s eye,  
Her mien like spring upon the hill:  
Perfection softly, passing by,  
“Behold the lass o’ Ballochmyle!”
source_text:
With careless step I onward stray’d,
My heart rejoic’d in nature’s joy,
When, musing in a lonely glade,
A maiden fair I chanc’d to spy:
Her look was like the morning’s eye,
Her air like nature’s vernal smile:
Perfection whisper’d, passing by,
“Behold the lass o’ Ballochmyle!”

text:
For you, not bred to barn or byre,
Who sweetly wake the Scottish lyre,
My thanks are due for all your line:
The marled plaid you kindly spare,
Should ever by me be prized with care;
It would delight me to the nine.

I’d be far prouder of my lot,
With gentle folds about me wrought,
Than any ermine ever wore,
Or proud imperial purple’s store.

Farewell then, long be hale then,
And plenty fill your fa;
May losses and crosses
Ne’er at your hallan ca’!
source_text:
For you, no bred to barn and byre,
Wha sweetly tune the Scottish lyre,
Thanks to you for your line:
The marled plaid ye kindly spare,
By me should gratefully be ware;
’Twad please me to the nine.
I’d be mair vauntie o’ my hap,
Douce hingin owre my curple,
Than ony ermine ever lap,
Or proud imperial purple.
Farewell then, lang hale then,
An’ plenty be your fa;
May losses and crosses
Ne’er at your hallan ca’!

text:
Already a casual species, rising quietly, showing up on the streets,  
People’s mouths praise only makers, lovers, fulfillers, those who know how to say yes,  
Soon there will be no more priests, I say their labor is finished,  
Death brings no crises here, but life is a continual state of crisis here,  
Are your body, your days, your manners, excellent? after death you will be excellent,  
Justice, health, self-respect, move through with unstoppable force;  
How dare you set anything above a human being?
source_text:
Already a nonchalant breed, silently emerging, appears on the streets,
People’s lips salute only doers, lovers, satisfiers, positive knowers,
There will shortly be no more priests, I say their work is done,
Death is without emergencies here, but life is perpetual emergencies here,
Are your body, days, manners, superb? after death you shall be superb,
Justice, health, self-esteem, clear the way with irresistible power;
How dare you place any thing before a man?

add:—

text:
And a few, like the ones we know,
Damned—but only God knows why—
Convinced their minds were made
To turn this ugly Hell to Heaven;
In that faith they live and die.
source_text:
And some few, like we know who,
Damned—but God alone knows why—
To believe their minds are given
To make this ugly Hell a Heaven;
In which faith they live and die.

text:
All thought God’s angel had come down to sweep  
The lingering guilty to their fire-lit grave;  
The Tyrant, from his throne, in terror leapt—  
Her innocence his child from fear still gave;  
Alarmed by the faith they merely feigned, the priestly slave  
Knelt for the mercy of the one they served with blood,  
And, like the ebbing of a mighty wave  
Drawn back into the roaring sea, the multitude  
With crushing panic fled, transformed by terror’s mood.
source_text:
All thought it was God’s Angel come to sweep
The lingering guilty to their fiery grave;
The Tyrant from his throne in dread did leap,—
Her innocence his child from fear did save;
Scared by the faith they feigned, each priestly slave
Knelt for his mercy whom they served with blood,
And, like the refluence of a mighty wave
Sucked into the loud sea, the multitude
With crushing panic, fled in terror’s altered mood.

text:
O such themes—equalities! O holy mean!  
Warblings beneath the sun, arriving now, or at noon, or in the fall of evening,  
Musical strains moving through the ages, now finding their way here,  
I turn to your wild and mingled chords, join them, and  
with cheerfulness carry them onward.
source_text:
O such themes--equalities! O divine average!
Warblings under the sun, usher’d as now, or at noon, or setting,
Strains musical flowing through ages, now reaching hither,
I take to your reckless and composite chords, add to them, and
cheerfully pass them forward.

text:
The climbing moon began to shine  
Above the far Cumnock hills to pine:  
To count her horns, with all my skill,  
I tried my best;  
Yet whether there were three or four,  
I could not tell.
source_text:
The rising moon began to glowre
The distant Cumnock hills out-owre:
To count her horns, wi’ a my pow’r,
I set mysel’;
But whether she had three or four,
I cou’d na tell.

text:
At least prayer remains, still remains.
O Jesus, in the air
I do not know which chamber is yours—
I’m knocking everywhere.
source_text:
At least to pray is left, is left.
O Jesus! in the air
I know not which thy chamber is, --
I 'm knocking everywhere.

text:
KING:
My lord Archbishop,
Do as you will, and all you can in this.
Your king on earth, and in heaven too,
Grants you wide power in his unsettled realm.
But we need money, and I fear me
For so great an enterprise, we are not yet supplied.
source_text:
KING:
My Lord Archbishop,
Do what thou wilt and what thou canst in this.
Thy earthly even as thy heavenly King
Gives thee large power in his unquiet realm.
But we want money, and my mind misgives me
That for so great an enterprise, as yet,
We are unfurnished.

text:
Beyond them lay the distant glades and the adjoining lawn,
and through the dark green press
the white sun shimmered like the dawn
beneath a speckled cloud.
source_text:
There lay far glades and neighbouring lawn,
And through the dark green crowd
The white sun twinkling like the dawn
Under a speckled cloud.

text:
Do you know the bright delights of youth?  
The joy of dear companions, of the laughing mouth and open face?  
The joy of a day all radiant with light, the joy of broad, free games?  
The joy of sweet music, of the lit ballroom and the dancers?  
The joy of a full table, a hard carouse, and drinking?
source_text:
Knowist thou the excellent joys of youth?
Joys of the dear companions and of the merry word and laughing face?
Joy of the glad light-beaming day, joy of the wide-breath’d games?
Joy of sweet music, joy of the lighted ball-room and the dancers?
Joy of the plenteous dinner, strong carouse and drinking?

text:
[Footnote 2: Another preacher, held as dear by the few,  
who, at that time, was suffering ill health. For him, see also “The Ordination,”  
stanza ix.—R.B.]
source_text:
[Footnote 2: Another preacher, an equal favourite with the few,
who was at that time ailing. For him see also “The Ordination,”
stanza ix.—R.B.]

text:
Has old Kilmarnock spied the fiend?
Or mighty Mackinlay turn askew and lean?
Or Robertson, made whole again, be seen,
To preach and read?
“Not worse than all!” cries every friend,
“Tam Samson’s dead!”
source_text:
Has auld Kilmarnock seen the deil?
Or great Mackinlay thrawn his heel?
Or Robertson again grown weel,
To preach an’ read?
“Na’ waur than a’!” cries ilka chiel,
“Tam Samson’s dead!”

text:
I woke and found it already awake,
I rose, and it came after me;
I tried to lose it in the crowd,
To leave it lost out in the sea,
source_text:
I waked, to find it first awake,
I rose, -- it followed me;
I tried to drop it in the crowd,
To lose it in the sea,

text:
In every shout that rises from a man,  
In every child’s small cry of fear,  
In every voice, in every law’s command,  
I hear the mind-forged shackles there:
source_text:
In every cry of every man,
In every infant’s cry of fear,
In every voice, in every ban,
The mind-forged manacles I hear:

text:
Set the flood free, and you’ll see it plain,
Wave on wave, held back for you;
Crimson trial! doubting Thomas,
Now, do you still deny your bird was true?
source_text:
Loose the flood, you shall find it patent,
Gush after gush, reserved for you;
Scarlet experiment! sceptic Thomas,
Now, do you doubt that your bird was true?

text:
Fat as the death-birds on Erin’s shore,  
that feasted deep on her dearest blood,  
and circled Castlereagh  
when they seized the Patriot’s heart, which his grip  
had torn from the widow’s crazed embrace,  
and fled before the break of day.
source_text:
Fat—as the Death-birds on Erin’s shore,
That glutted themselves in her dearest gore,
And flitted round Castlereagh,
When they snatched the Patriot’s heart, that HIS grasp
Had torn from its widow’s maniac clasp,
—And fled at the dawn of day.

text:
Chorus—O good ale comes, and good ale goes;
Good ale makes me sell my hose,
Sell my hose, and pawn my shoon—
Good ale keeps my heart aboon!
source_text:
Chorus—O gude ale comes and gude ale goes;
Gude ale gars me sell my hose,
Sell my hose, and pawn my shoon—
Gude ale keeps my heart aboon!

text:
“Play a song about a lamb!”
So I played it, bright and glad.
“Piper, play that song again.”
So I played; he wept to hear.
source_text:
‘Pipe a song about a Lamb!’
So I piped with merry cheer.
‘Piper, pipe that song again.’
So I piped: he wept to hear.

text:
I can wade through sorrow,  
whole pools of it—  
I know that well.  
But the slightest touch of joy  
breaks my footing,  
and I reel, drunk.  
Let no pebble grin;  
it was the fresh liquor—  
that was all.
source_text:
I can wade grief,
Whole pools of it, --
I 'm used to that.
But the least push of joy
Breaks up my feet,
And I tip -- drunken.
Let no pebble smile,
'T was the new liquor, --
That was all!

text:
For ye that are sae douce, ye mock at this;
Ye’re nothing but blind, foolish asses, O:
The wisest man that e’er the world beheld
Was dearly fond of bonnie lasses, O.
Green grow, &c.
source_text:
For you sae douce, ye sneer at this;
Ye’re nought but senseless asses, O:
The wisest man the warl’ e’er saw,
He dearly lov’d the lasses, O.
Green grow, &c.

text:
FIRST FURY:
We are the agents of pain, of dread,
Of bitterness, suspicion, and of hate,
And of the crimes that cling and will not die; and like
Lean hounds pursuing some struck, crying fawn
Through wood and water, we hunt all who weep,
Who bleed, who breathe, when the great King yields them up to us.
source_text:
FIRST FURY:
We are the ministers of pain, and fear,
And disappointment, and mistrust, and hate,
And clinging crime; and as lean dogs pursue
Through wood and lake some struck and sobbing fawn,
We track all things that weep, and bleed, and live,
When the great King betrays them to our will.

text:
Away with worn-out romance!
Away with novels, plots, and dramas of distant courts,
Away with love poems sweetened into rhyme, the schemes and affairs of the idle,
Meant only for night banquets where dancers drift to languid music,
The unwholesome pleasures, the costly excesses of the few,
With perfume, heat, and wine beneath the glare of chandeliers.
source_text:
Away with old romance!
Away with novels, plots and plays of foreign courts,
Away with love-verses sugar’d in rhyme, the intrigues, amours of idlers,
Fitted for only banquets of the night where dancers to late music slide,
The unhealthy pleasures, extravagant dissipations of the few,
With perfumes, heat and wine, beneath the dazzling chandeliers.

text:
Now, like a mighty wind, they lift the voice of song to heaven,
Or like harmonious thunder rolling through the courts of heaven:
Beneath them sit the aged men, wise guardians of the poor.
Then cherish pity, lest you turn an angel from your door.
source_text:
Now like a mighty wind they raise to heaven the voice of song,
Or like harmonious thunderings the seats of heaven among:
Beneath them sit the aged men, wise guardians of the poor.
Then cherish pity, lest you drive an angel from your door.

text:
In this moment, longing and reflective, sitting alone,  
it seems to me there are other men in other lands, longing and reflective;  
it seems to me I can turn and see them in Germany, Italy,  
France, Spain,  
or far, far off, in China, or in Russia, or speaking other tongues,  
and it seems to me that if I could know those men, I would grow  
attached to them as I am to men in my own country,  
O I know we would be brothers and lovers,  
I know I would be glad in their company.
source_text:
This moment yearning and thoughtful sitting alone,
It seems to me there are other men in other lands yearning and thoughtful,
It seems to me I can look over and behold them in Germany, Italy,
France, Spain,
Or far, far away, in China, or in Russia or talking other dialects,
And it seems to me if I could know those men I should become
attached to them as I do to men in my own lands,
O I know we should be brethren and lovers,
I know I should be happy with them.

text:
Chanting the sacred square, advancing from the One, from all its sides,  
From the old and the new, from the square wholly divine,  
Solid, four-sided, with every side required, from this face Jehovah I am,  
Old Brahm I, and Saturnius too am I;  
Time does not touch me—I am Time, ancient, as current as any,  
Unmoved by persuasion, without mercy, delivering just decrees,  
Like the Earth, the Father, the dark old Kronos, bound to law,  
Aged beyond reckoning, yet never changed, still turning those vast laws,  
I spare no one—whoever sins must die—I will demand that life;  
So let no one look for mercy—do the seasons, gravity, the  
Set days, show mercy? no more do I,  
But like the seasons and gravity, and like all the appointed days  
That grant no pardon,  
From this side I send forth judgments unyielding, without the least regret.
source_text:
Chanting the square deific, out of the One advancing, out of the sides,
Out of the old and new, out of the square entirely divine,
Solid, four-sided, (all the sides needed,) from this side Jehovah am I,
Old Brahm I, and I Saturnius am;
Not Time affects me--I am Time, old, modern as any,
Unpersuadable, relentless, executing righteous judgments,
As the Earth, the Father, the brown old Kronos, with laws,
Aged beyond computation, yet never new, ever with those mighty laws rolling,
Relentless I forgive no man--whoever sins dies--I will have that man’s life;
Therefore let none expect mercy--have the seasons, gravitation, the
appointed days, mercy? no more have I,
But as the seasons and gravitation, and as all the appointed days
that forgive not,
I dispense from this side judgments inexorable without the least remorse.

text:
Awa, ye greedy, warldly race,
That think all havins, sense, and grace,
Ay, even love and friendship, space
Should yield—to grab the plack!
I canna thole to see your face,
Nor hear your crack.
source_text:
Awa ye selfish, war’ly race,
Wha think that havins, sense, an’ grace,
Ev’n love an’ friendship should give place
To catch—the—plack!
I dinna like to see your face,
Nor hear your crack.

text:
Then come home, my children, the sun has gone down,  
And the night dews are rising high;  
Come, come, put aside your play, and let us be away,  
Till the morning opens in the sky.
source_text:
‘Then come home, my children, the sun is gone down,
And the dews of night arise;
Come, come, leave off play, and let us away,
Till the morning appears in the skies.’

text:
The Cooper o’ Cuddy came here away,
He called the girrs right o’er us a’;
And our gudewife has gotten a ca’,
That’s angered the silly gudeman O.
We’ll hide the Cooper, &c.
source_text:
The Cooper o’ Cuddy came here awa,
He ca’d the girrs out o’er us a’;
An’ our gudewife has gotten a ca’,
That’s anger’d the silly gudeman O.
We’ll hide the Cooper, &c.

text:
These dainties heaped he with a glowing hand  
On golden platters and in baskets bright  
Of twisting silver: lavishly they stand  
Within the hush and shelter of the night,  
Suffusing the cold room with fragrance light.--  
“And now, my love, my seraph, waking fair!  
Thou art my heaven, and I am thine eremite:  
Lift up thine eyes, for gentle St. Agnes’ prayer,  
Or I shall sink in sleep beside thee, sore my soul with care.”
source_text:
These delicates he heap'd with glowing hand
On golden dishes and in baskets bright
Of wreathed silver: sumptuous they stand
In the retired quiet of the night,
Filling the chilly room with perfume light.--
"And now, my love, my seraph fair, awake!
Thou art my heaven, and I thine eremite:
Open thine eyes, for meek St. Agnes' sake,
Or I shall drowse beside thee, so my soul doth ache."

text:
The sun sinks down in the west,  
The evening star is bright;  
The birds are quiet in their nest,  
And I must find my night.  
The moon, like a blossom  
In heaven’s lofty awning,  
With still delight,  
Sits smiling through the night.
source_text:
The sun descending in the West,
The evening star does shine;
The birds are silent in their nest,
And I must seek for mine.
The moon, like a flower
In heaven’s high bower,
With silent delight,
Sits and smiles on the night.

text:
I had come round by the hill,
And toddled down toward Willie’s mill,
Planting my staff with all my skill,
To keep me steady;
Though leeward still, against my will,
I lurched already.
source_text:
I was come round about the hill,
An’ todlin down on Willie’s mill,
Setting my staff wi’ a’ my skill,
To keep me sicker;
Tho’ leeward whiles, against my will,
I took a bicker.

text:
What flock with Moodie’s flock could vie?—
So sound in limb, so brisk and spry!
No poisoned, sour Arminian reek
He ever gave to them to drink;
From Calvin’s well, still clear and bright,
They quaffed—O feast of pure delight!
source_text:
What flock wi’ Moodie’s flock could rank?—
Sae hale and hearty every shank!
Nae poison’d soor Arminian stank
He let them taste;
Frae Calvin’s well, aye clear, drank,—
O, sic a feast!

text:
The modest rose still bears a thorn,  
The gentle sheep a threatening horn:  
But the white lily lives in love’s delight,  
And neither thorn nor threat can mar her shining light.
source_text:
The modest Rose puts forth a thorn,
The humble sheep a threat’ning horn:
While the Lily white shall in love delight,
Nor a thorn nor a threat stain her beauty bright.

text:
Not from love’s success alone,  
Nor wealth, nor honored middle years, nor triumphs won in politics or war;  
But when life thins and all the storming passions settle,  
As rich, misted, wordless colors wash the evening sky,  
As ease, abundance, and repose soak through the body, like newer, sweeter air,  
As the days deepen into a gentler light, and the apple finally hangs  
truly complete, and lazily ripe, upon the tree,  
Then comes the busiest quiet, the happiest days of all—  
the meditative, radiant halcyon days.
source_text:
Not from successful love alone,
Nor wealth, nor honor’d middle age, nor victories of politics or war;
But as life wanes, and all the turbulent passions calm,
As gorgeous, vapory, silent hues cover the evening sky,
As softness, fulness, rest, suffuse the frame, like freshier, balmier air,
As the days take on a mellower light, and the apple at last hangs
really finish’d and indolent-ripe on the tree,
Then for the teeming quietest, happiest days of all!
The brooding and blissful halcyon days!

text:
But this I’m gaun to tell ye true,  
That late one night befell me too,  
Is just as true as Deil in hell,  
Or Dublin city:  
That ever he should near us come  
Is muckle pity.
source_text:
But this that I am gaun to tell,
Which lately on a night befell,
Is just as true’s the Deil’s in hell
Or Dublin city:
That e’er he nearer comes oursel’
’S a muckle pity.

text:
My girl, I set this meeting with you, and I ask you to prepare yourself  
to be worthy of the moment when I come to you,  
and I ask you to remain patient and whole until I arrive.
source_text:
My girl I appoint with you an appointment, and I charge you that you
make preparation to be worthy to meet me,
And I charge you that you be patient and perfect till I come.

text:
O Melancholy, stay with us a while!  
O Music, Music, breathe in mournful wise!  
O Echo, Echo, from some dusky isle,  
Unkown, Lethean, come and mourn and sigh—  
Sigh to our hearts, and sorrow into smile;  
Lift up your heads, dear Spirits, gravely wise,  
And cast a pallid radiance through the shade,  
With silver wanly touching tombs of jade.
source_text:
O Melancholy, linger here awhile!
O Music, Music, breathe despondingly!
O Echo, Echo, from some sombre isle,
Unknown, Lethean, sigh to us--O sigh!
Spirits in grief, lift up your heads, and smile;
Lift up your heads, sweet Spirits, heavily,
And make a pale light in your cypress glooms,
Tinting with silver wan your marble tombs.

text:
Read then of faith  
That burned above the bundle of sticks;  
Bright notes of hymn  
The river could not silence;  
Bold names of men  
And heavenly women,  
Passed out of record  
Into renown!
source_text:
Read then of faith
That shone above the fagot;
Clear strains of hymn
The river could not drown;
Brave names of men
And celestial women,
Passed out of record
Into renown!

text:
Once here the Stuarts, crowned with splendor, reigned,  
And framed the laws by which Scotland’s good was gained;  
But now their palace stands without a roof above,  
And other hands have seized the sceptre’s haughty move.  
Down fallen indeed, to earth’s low dust they’ve turned,  
From which the creeping reptiles first are borne and spurned.  
The wronged Stuart line has perished, passed away,  
And strangers now possess the throne they used to sway;  
A witless race, from honour’s path removed and lost,  
And those who know them best despise them most.

I’ll shelter him from all the burning heat
Until he can with joy rest on our Father’s knee;
Then I will rise and smooth his silver hair,
And be like him, and then he’ll love me.
source_text:
I’ll shade him from the heat till he can bear
To lean in joy upon our Father’s knee;
And then I’ll stand and stroke his silver hair,
And be like him, and he will then love me.

text:
Oh, how shall I sing myself for the dead one I loved there?
And how shall I dress my song for the great, sweet soul that has passed?
And what perfume shall I bring to the grave of the one I love?
source_text:
O how shall I warble myself for the dead one there I loved?
And how shall I deck my song for the large sweet soul that has gone?
And what shall my perfume be for the grave of him I love?

text:
A load o’ trouble, should that chance us all,
A night o’ kind fellowship softens it baith:
And when at the blythe close o’ our journey at last,
Wha the deil ever minds the road he has past?
Contented wi’ little, &c.
source_text:
A townmond o’ trouble, should that be may fa’,
A night o’ gude fellowship sowthers it a’:
When at the blythe end o’ our journey at last,
Wha the deil ever thinks o’ the road he has past?
Contented wi’ little, &c.

text:
And there, the lion’s glowing eyes  
Will spill with tears of gold:  
And, pitying the soft, sad cries,  
It circles slowly round the fold:  
Saying: “By His gentleness, wrath,  
And, by His healing, sickness,  
Is driven away  
From our immortal day.”
source_text:
And there the lion’s ruddy eyes
Shall flow with tears of gold:
And pitying the tender cries,
And walking round the fold:
Saying: ‘Wrath by His meekness,
And, by His health, sickness,
Is driven away
From our immortal day.

text:
And so this cherished child could feel the pull  
Of all my imaginings, massing like a cloud  
On the very wind that carries them away:  
Her thoughts were mine as well, before they took  
On music and on light, and from their source  
Broke forth in poetry; and her still, intent face,  
Pale with the feelings burning bright within,  
Was turned to mine with a grace beyond words,  
Watching the hopes her heart had already learned to follow.
source_text:
And this beloved child thus felt the sway
Of my conceptions, gathering like a cloud
The very wind on which it rolls away:
Hers too were all my thoughts, ere yet, endowed
With music and with light, their fountains flowed
In poesy; and her still and earnest face,
Pallid with feelings which intensely glowed
Within, was turned on mine with speechless grace,
Watching the hopes which there her heart had learned to trace.

text:
Life, and Death, and Giants
Are still the same.
Small devices, hopper of the mill,
Beetle by the candle,
Or a fife’s slight renown,
Hold, by chance alone,
The thing they sound aloud.
source_text:
Life, and Death, and Giants
Such as these, are still.
Minor apparatus, hopper of the mill,
Beetle at the candle,
Or a fife's small fame,
Maintain by accident
That they proclaim.

text:
I saw their famished lips at dusk  
With dreadful warning opened wide,  
Then I awoke, and found myself  
Upon the cold hill’s side.
source_text:
I saw their starv'd lips in the gloam
With horrid warning gaped wide,
And I awoke, and found me here
On the cold hill's side.

text:
We never know we are leaving—when we go  
We laugh and close the door;  
Fate, close behind us, locks it fast,  
And we speak there never more.
source_text:
We never know we go, -- when we are going
We jest and shut the door;
Fate following behind us bolts it,
And we accost no more.

text:
So, meaning each brief hour to steal away,  
She yet delayed. Meanwhile, o'er the moorlands bare,  
Young Porphyro had come, with burning heart alway  
For Madeline. By the great portal there,  
Moon-shadowed from the light, he takes his stand, and prayer  
Lifts up to all the saints for sight of her,  
If only once amid those weary hours, and dare  
To gaze and worship, hidden from the stir;  
Perchance to speak, to kneel, to touch, to kiss—such things still were.
source_text:
So, purposing each moment to retire,
She linger'd still. Meantime, across the moors,
Had come young Porphyro, with heart on fire
For Madeline. Beside the portal doors,
Buttress'd from moonlight, stands he, and implores
All saints to give him sight of Madeline,
But for one moment in the tedious hours,
That he might gaze and worship all unseen;
Perchance speak, kneel, touch, kiss--in sooth such things
have been.

text:
The Devil he pledged by the edge of his knife,  
Hey, and the rue blooms fair with thyme;  
He pitied the man who was bound to a wife,  
And the thyme is now withered, and rue is in prime.
source_text:
The Devil he swore by the edge o’ his knife,
Hey, and the rue grows bonie wi’ thyme;
He pitied the man that was tied to a wife,
And the thyme it is wither’d, and rue is in prime.

text:
Upon this casement shone the winter moon,
And cast warm crimson on Madeline’s fair breast,
As down she knelt to seek from heaven its boon;
Rose-color lay on hands together pressed,
And on her silver cross a soft amethyst,
And on her hair a glory, like a saint:
She seemed a radiant angel newly dressed,
Save for the wings, fit for the heavens; Porphyro grew faint:
She knelt, so pure a thing, so free from earthly taint.
source_text:
Full on this casement shone the wintry moon,
And threw warm gules on Madeline's fair breast,
As down she knelt for heaven's grace and boon;
Rose-bloom fell on her hands, together prest,
And on her silver cross soft amethyst,
And on her hair a glory, like a saint:
She seem'd a splendid angel, newly drest,
Save wings, for heaven:--Porphyro grew faint:
She knelt, so pure a thing, so free from mortal taint.

text:
How was it that these same account-men could espy  
Fair Isabella in her soft and downy nest?  
How could they mark in Lorenzo’s eye  
A wandering from his labor? Hot Egypt’s pest  
Crept into their covetous, sly sight!  
How could these money-bags look east and west?--  
Yet so they did--and every honest dealer  
Must look behind, as does the hunted hare.
source_text:
How was it these same ledger-men could spy
Fair Isabella in her downy nest?
How could they find out in Lorenzo's eye
A straying from his toil? Hot Egypt's pest
Into their vision covetous and sly!
How could these money-bags see east and west?--
Yet so they did--and every dealer fair
Must see behind, as doth the hunted hare.

text:
And their sun will never glow,
And their fields lie stark and bare,
And their paths are choked with thorns,
There is winter always there.
source_text:
And their sun does never shine,
And their fields are bleak and bare,
And their ways are filled with thorns,
It is eternal winter there.

text:
“This day we mean, this very hour, to ride  
Three leagues away, upon the Apennine;  
Come down, we pray thee, ere the burning tide  
Of sun shall tell his dewy beads on vine and eglantine.”

Lorenzo, as his courtesy was still,  
Bent to that serpent-whisper with a sign;  
Then swiftly went, in haste to make him fit,  
With belt, and spur, and huntsman’s gear made tight.
source_text:
"To-day we purpose, ay, this hour we mount
To spur three leagues towards the Apennine;
Come down, we pray thee, ere the hot sun count
His dewy rosary on the eglantine."
Lorenzo, courteously as he was wont,
Bow'd a fair greeting to these serpents' whine;
And went in haste, to get in readiness,
With belt, and spur, and bracing huntsman's dress.

text:
No festive soldiers—young, and yet already veterans,  
Rugged, dark-skinned, handsome, strong, drawn from homestead and workshop,  
Seared by many a long campaign and sweat-soaked march,  
Tempered on many a hard-won, bloodied field.
source_text:
No holiday soldiers--youthful, yet veterans,
Worn, swart, handsome, strong, of the stock of homestead and workshop,
Harden’d of many a long campaign and sweaty march,
Inured on many a hard-fought bloody field.

text:
I think I could turn and live among animals; they are so calm and
self-contained,
I stand and watch them, long and longer still.
source_text:
I think I could turn and live with animals, they are so placid and
self-contain’d,
I stand and look at them long and long.

text:
And it grew by day and night,
Till it bore an apple bright,
And my foe beheld it gleam,
And he knew that it was mine,—
source_text:
And it grew both day and night,
Till it bore an apple bright,
And my foe beheld it shine,
And he knew that it was mine,—

text:
Saying, “Isabel, my gentle sweet!
Red whortle-berries hang and bow above my brow,
And a great flint stone burdens still my feet;
About me beeches and tall chestnuts sow
Their leaves and prickly nuts; a sheepfold’s bleat
Floats from beyond the river to my bed:
Go, let one tear fall on my heather bloom,
And it shall comfort me within the tomb.
source_text:
Saying moreover, "Isabel, my sweet!
Red whortle-berries droop above my head,
And a large flint-stone weighs upon my feet;
Around me beeches and high chestnuts shed
Their leaves and prickly nuts; a sheep-fold bleat
Comes from beyond the river to my bed:
Go, shed one tear upon my heather-bloom,
And it shall comfort me within the tomb.

text:
Tell me how many notes there are
In the new robin’s rapture
Among the startled boughs;
How many journeys the tortoise takes,
How many cups the bee partakes, --
That reveler in the dew!
source_text:
Write me how many notes there be
In the new robin's ecstasy
Among astonished boughs;
How many trips the tortoise makes,
How many cups the bee partakes, --
The debauchee of dews!

text:
Thou still untouched bride of quietness,
Thou child of silence, nurtured by slow time,
Woodland historian, who can thus express
A flowered tale more sweetly than our rhyme:
What leaf-edged legend lingers round thy form
Of gods or mortals, or of both together,
In Tempe or the vales of Arcady?
What men or gods are these? What maidens loath?
What frantic chase? What struggle to flee?
What pipes and timbrels? What wild ecstasy?
source_text:
Thou still unravish'd bride of quietness,
Thou foster-child of silence and slow time,
Sylvan historian, who canst thus express
A flowery tale more sweetly than our rhyme:
What leaf-fring'd legend haunts about thy shape
Of deities or mortals, or of both,
In Tempe or the dales of Arcady?
What men or gods are these? What maidens loth?
What mad pursuit? What struggle to escape?
What pipes and timbrels? What wild ecstasy?

text:
I had a scarlet robin  
Who sang through many a day,  
But when the woods turned golden,  
He, too, flew off away.  
Time brought me other robins,  
Their songs were just the same,  
Yet for my lost troubadour  
I kept the house at home.
source_text:
I had a crimson robin
Who sang full many a day,
But when the woods were painted
He, too, did fly away.
Time brought me other robins, --
Their ballads were the same, --
Still for my missing troubadour
I kept the 'house at hame.'

text:
And he wad do their errands weel,  
And muckle he wad say;  
And ilka ane about the court  
Wad gie him gude-day aye.
source_text:
And he wad do their errands weel,
And meikle he wad say;
And ilka ane about the court
Wad bid to him gude-day.

text:
Hark! from faerie land there comes a storm of elves,  
With withered looks it seems, yet grants a kindly boon:  
Awake—awake! the dawn is near;  
The swollen revelers will never heed our flight:  
Come let us go, my love, with glad and swift delight;  
No ears are there to listen, no eyes to watch us now,  
All drowned in Rhenish and the drowsy mead:  
Awake! arise, my love, and fear no harm,  
For over the southern moors I have a home for thee.
source_text:
"Hark! 'tis an elfin-storm from faery land,
Of haggard seeming, but a boon indeed:
Arise--arise! the morning is at hand;--
The bloated wassaillers will never heed:--
Let us away, my love, with happy speed;
There are no ears to hear, or eyes to see,--
Drown'd all in Rhenish and the sleepy mead:
Awake! arise! my love, and fearless be,
For o'er the southern moors I have a home for thee."

text:
As down the stream they made their way,  
And through the blooming vale;  
His cheek to hers he oft would lay,  
And love was still the tale:
source_text:
As down the burn they took their way,
And thro’ the flowery dale;
His cheek to hers he aft did lay,
And love was aye the tale:

text:
And “Do you fear?” and “Do you fear?”  
And “Do you see?” and “Do you hear?”  
And “Are we not driving free  
Over the dreadful sea,  
You and I?”
source_text:
And ‘Fear’st thou?’ and ‘Fear’st thou?’
And Seest thou?’ and ‘Hear’st thou?’
And ‘Drive we not free
O’er the terrible sea,
I and thou?’

text:
Read, dear, how others struggled,
until we are stronger;
what they gave up,
until we fear less;
how many times they carried
the faithful witness,
until we are aided,
as if a kingdom took notice!
source_text:
Read, sweet, how others strove,
Till we are stouter;
What they renounced,
Till we are less afraid;
How many times they bore
The faithful witness,
Till we are helped,
As if a kingdom cared!

text:
You were not made for death, bright immortal Bird!  
No ravenous ages trample you to dust;  
The sound I hear this fleeting night was heard  
Long ago by king and beggar, in equal trust:  
Perhaps the very same sweet strain that found  
Its way through Ruth’s sad heart, when, home-longing, she  
Stood weeping in the stranger’s fields of grain;  
The same that often has  
Enchanted magic windows, flung wide to the foam  
Of dangerous seas, in forlorn fairy lands.
source_text:
Thou wast not born for death, immortal Bird!
No hungry generations tread thee down;
The voice I hear this passing night was heard
In ancient days by emperor and clown:
Perhaps the self-same song that found a path
Through the sad heart of Ruth, when, sick for home,
She stood in tears amid the alien corn;
The same that oft-times hath
Charm'd magic casements, opening on the foam
Of perilous seas, in faery lands forlorn.

text:
It cannot be my spirit,
For it was yours before;
I yielded up all that I knew of dust, --
What richer thing, then,
Could I possess, a lowly maiden,
Whose highest reach
Was only that I might,
In some far heaven,
Dwell shyly there with you!
source_text:
It cannot be my spirit,
For that was thine before;
I ceded all of dust I knew, --
What opulence the more
Had I, a humble maiden,
Whose farthest of degree
Was that she might,
Some distant heaven,
Dwell timidly with thee!

text:
I said I’d won it, —
That was all.
See how I hold it tight,
For fear it fall,
And leave me poor once more;
One fleeting mercy’s touch has made me
Unfit to wear the beggar’s face
I wore an hour before.
source_text:
I said I gained it, --
This was all.
Look, how I clutch it,
Lest it fall,
And I a pauper go;
Unfitted by an instant's grace
For the contented beggar's face
I wore an hour ago.

text:
Oh, my friend,  
my sister, my beloved—what can I even say?  
My thoughts are reeling, and I can barely know  
whether I am speaking to you or to her.
source_text:
INDIAN:
Oh! my friend,
My sister, my beloved!—What do I say?
My brain is dizzy, and I scarce know whether
I speak to thee or her.

text:
‘I have no name;
I am only two days old.’
What shall I call you?
‘I am full of joy,
Joy is my name.’
May sweet joy be yours!
source_text:
‘I have no name;
I am but two days old.’
What shall I call thee?
‘I happy am,
Joy is my name.’
Sweet joy befall thee!

text:
Men claim they have seen God, and heard him speak,  
Or learned of him through others who have known;  
And that his will is all our law, a whip  
To scourge us into slaves—that Priests and Kings
source_text:
8 7 1-4:
Men say they have seen God, and heard from God,
Or known from others who have known such things,
And that his will is all our law, a rod
To scourge us into slaves—that Priests and Kings

text:
A woman’s body on the auction block,  
she too is more than herself—she is the crowded mother of mothers,  
the one who bears those yet to grow and become mates to the mothers.
source_text:
A woman’s body at auction,
She too is not only herself, she is the teeming mother of mothers,
She is the bearer of them that shall grow and be mates to the mothers.

text:
“Do you not hear the Aziola cry?
I think she must be near,”
said Mary, as we sat
in dusk before the stars were out, before the candles came;
and I, who thought
this Aziola some tiresome woman,
asked, “Who is Aziola?” How relieved
I was to learn it was not human,
no taunt of me to dread or despise:
and Mary saw my heart
and laughed, then said, “Do not trouble yourself;
it is only a small, soft owl.”
source_text:
‘Do you not hear the Aziola cry?
Methinks she must be nigh,’
Said Mary, as we sate
In dusk, ere stars were lit, or candles brought;
And I, who thought
This Aziola was some tedious woman,
Asked, ‘Who is Aziola?’ How elate
I felt to know that it was nothing human,
No mockery of myself to fear or hate:
And Mary saw my soul,
And laughed, and said, ‘Disquiet yourself not;
’Tis nothing but a little downy owl.’

text:
In each, the poet gazes on life as it is,  
and on the changeless law of beauty—  
in the first three, seeing them set in stern contrast;  
in the last, bringing them into harmony,  
and leaving us content.

He enters there: let not a humming whisper speak;  
Let every eye be veiled, or else a hundred swords  
Will batter down his heart, that fevered keep of Love:  
For him those rooms contain barbarian hordes,  
Hyena enemies, and fiery lords,  
Whose very hounds would send out curses loud  
Against his bloodline: not one breast accords  
Him any pity in that loathsome house,  
Save one old beldame, frail in flesh and soul.
source_text:
He ventures in: let no buzz'd whisper tell:
All eyes be muffled, or a hundred swords
Will storm his heart, Love's fev'rous citadel:
For him, those chambers held barbarian hordes,
Hyena foemen, and hot-blooded lords,
Whose very dogs would execrations howl
Against his lineage: not one breast affords
Him any mercy, in that mansion foul,
Save one old beldame, weak in body and in soul.

text:
Till the little ones grow weary,  
And can be merry no more,  
The sun is sinking lower,  
And our games are finished now.  
About their mothers’ laps  
Many sisters and brothers,  
Like birds within their nest,  
Are ready now for rest,  
And no more sport is seen  
On the meadow turning dim.
source_text:
Till the little ones, weary,
No more can be merry:
The sun does descend,
And our sports have an end.
Round the laps of their mothers
Many sisters and brothers,
Like birds in their nest,
Are ready for rest,
And sport no more seen
On the darkening green.

text:
What ground is under siege, and stumbles in a failed attempt to break it?
Look, I send to that ground a commander—swift, fearless, undying—
with cavalry and infantry, and whole batteries of cannon,
and gunners more lethal than any who ever touched a fuse.
source_text:
What place is besieged, and vainly tries to raise the siege?
Lo, I send to that place a commander, swift, brave, immortal,
And with him horse and foot, and parks of artillery,
And artillery-men, the deadliest that ever fired gun.

text:
The garden, once lovely, turned chill and unclean,
Like the body of her who had been its heart unseen,
Which first lay beautiful, as though wrapped in sleep,
Then little by little began to steep
Into a heap that made even the unweeping weep.
source_text:
The garden, once fair, became cold and foul,
Like the corpse of her who had been its soul,
Which at first was lovely as if in sleep,
Then slowly changed, till it grew a heap
To make men tremble who never weep.

text:
And as he went across the court-yard’s space,  
At every third slow step he paused to heed  
If he might catch his lady’s matin-lay,  
Or hear the gentle whisper of her tread;  
And, while he thus delayed beneath love’s chase,  
A laugh came sounding sweetly overhead;  
Then, looking up, he saw her radiant face  
Smile through the inward lattice, glad and bright.
source_text:
And as he to the court-yard pass'd along,
Each third step did he pause, and listen'd oft
If he could hear his lady's matin-song,
Or the light whisper of her footstep soft;
And as he thus over his passion hung,
He heard a laugh full musical aloft;
When, looking up, he saw her features bright
Smile through an in-door lattice, all delight.

text:
Though surely this was Hell itself;
he was the Devil, and they all
what if the claret moved so smooth,
and wit, like tides, came up and fell?—
were damned forever still.
source_text:
Though to be sure this place was Hell;
He was the Devil—and all they—
What though the claret circled well,
And wit, like ocean, rose and fell?—
Were damned eternally.

text:
Who has not marked thee often in thy plenty?
At times, who wanders forth may chance to find
Thee seated heedless on the granary floor,
Thy hair, made light, stirred softly by the winnowing wind;
Or on a half-cut furrow lying fast asleep,
Made drowsy by the steam of poppies, while thy hook
Spares the next swath and all its braided flowers:
And sometimes, like a gleaner, thou dost keep
With steady step thy burdened head across a brook;
Or by a cider-press, with patient gaze,
Thou watchest the final droppings hour by hour.
source_text:
Who hath not seen thee oft amid thy store?
Sometimes whoever seeks abroad may find
Thee sitting careless on a granary floor,
Thy hair soft-lifted by the winnowing wind;
Or on a half-reap'd furrow sound asleep,
Drows'd with the fume of poppies, while thy hook
Spares the next swath and all its twined flowers:
And sometimes like a gleaner thou dost keep
Steady thy laden head across a brook;
Or by a cyder-press, with patient look,
Thou watchest the last oozings hours by hours.

text:
BOYS:
O bliss! O dread! what can be done
When the sun is gone from sight?
Come, then!
Let sleep’s bright gates swing wide!
When vigor and beauty join as one,
Their likeness sparks like a star
Across a sea of polished weather.
Away, shy hour, and darken now,
Lest eyes behold their own delight!
Away, fleet hour, and in your flight
Return again and yet again.
source_text:
BOYS:
O joy! O fear! what may be done
In the absence of the sun?
Come along!
The golden gates of sleep unbar!
When strength and beauty meet together,
Kindles their image like a star
In a sea of glassy weather.
Hence, coy hour! and quench thy light,
Lest eyes see their own delight!
Hence, swift hour! and thy loved flight
Oft renew.

text:
The sun hung low in the west on a winter day,
When through a narrow aisle among the thieves and outlaws of the land,
(There, by the hundreds seated, scarred murderers, sly counterfeiters,
Gathered for Sunday worship within prison walls, guards all around,
Many, well armed, standing watch with vigilant eyes,)
A lady walked in calm, holding a small innocent child by either hand,
And seating them upon their stools beside her on the platform,
She, first giving the instrument a soft, musical prelude,
Then, with a voice beyond all others, sang a strange old hymn.
source_text:
The sun was low in the west one winter day,
When down a narrow aisle amid the thieves and outlaws of the land,
(There by the hundreds seated, sear-faced murderers, wily counterfeiters,
Gather’d to Sunday church in prison walls, the keepers round,
Plenteous, well-armed, watching with vigilant eyes,)
Calmly a lady walk’d holding a little innocent child by either hand,
Whom seating on their stools beside her on the platform,
She, first preluding with the instrument a low and musical prelude,
In voice surpassing all, sang forth a quaint old hymn.

text:
O radiant, crowning, jubilant song!
A force beyond the earth sounds in your notes,
Processions of triumph—humankind set free—the conqueror at last,
Praises to the boundless God from all mankind—all joy!
A newborn race rises—a world made whole, all joy!
Women and men in wisdom, innocence, and health—all joy!
Wild laughing revelers overflowing with joy!
War, grief, and suffering ended—the heavy earth cleansed—nothing but joy remains!
The ocean brimmed with joy—the air itself all joy!
Joy! joy! in freedom, worship, love! joy in the rapture of life!
To simply be is enough! enough to breathe!
Joy! joy! everywhere, all joy!
source_text:
O glad, exulting, culminating song!
A vigor more than earth’s is in thy notes,
Marches of victory--man disenthral’d--the conqueror at last,
Hymns to the universal God from universal man--all joy!
A reborn race appears--a perfect world, all joy!
Women and men in wisdom innocence and health--all joy!
Riotous laughing bacchanals fill’d with joy!
War, sorrow, suffering gone--the rank earth purged--nothing but joy left!
The ocean fill’d with joy--the atmosphere all joy!
Joy! joy! in freedom, worship, love! joy in the ecstasy of life!
Enough to merely be! enough to breathe!
Joy! joy! all over joy!

text:
I drift along each mapped-out street,  
Beside the mapped-out Thames below,  
A sign on every face I meet,  
Signs of frailty, signs of woe.
source_text:
I wander through each chartered street,
Near where the chartered Thames does flow,
A mark in every face I meet,
Marks of weakness, marks of woe.

text:
Think ye that folk like you and I,
Wha toil and tramp through wet and dry,
Wi’ labor never done;
Think ye, are we less blest than those,
Wha scarce give heed as by us goes,
As though we mattered none?
Alas! how oft in proud disdain
God’s creatures they abase!
Or else, despising a’ that’s clean,
They revel in disgrace!
Baith reckless and heedless
Of heaven above, or hell below;
Doubting and scorning
It’s all but wind and show!
source_text:
Think ye, that sic as you and I,
Wha drudge an’ drive thro’ wet and dry,
Wi’ never-ceasing toil;
Think ye, are we less blest than they,
Wha scarcely tent us in their way,
As hardly worth their while?
Alas! how aft in haughty mood,
God’s creatures they oppress!
Or else, neglecting a’ that’s guid,
They riot in excess!
Baith careless and fearless
Of either heaven or hell;
Esteeming and deeming
It’s a’ an idle tale!

text:
A blazing radiance crowned his head,  
Fire thickened all the storm-dark air,  
With savage cadence he summoned the dead,  
And there they stood, a ragged, varied throng.—
source_text:
A burning brilliance on his head,
Flaming filled the stormy air,
In a wild verse he called the dead,
The dead in motley crowd were there.—

text:
In what far-off depths or skies  
Did the fire within your eyes ignite?  
On what wings would he dare to rise?  
What hand would dare to grasp that light?
source_text:
In what distant deeps or skies
Burnt the fire of thine eyes?
On what wings dare he aspire?
What the hand dare seize the fire?

text:
O, what a multitude they seemed, these flowers of London town!
Grouped in their companies, they sat, each bright with light of their own.
The murmur of the crowd was there, yet it was like a crowd of lambs,
Thousands of little boys and girls lifting their innocent hands.
source_text:
O what a multitude they seemed, these flowers of London town!
Seated in companies they sit, with radiance all their own.
The hum of multitudes was there, but multitudes of lambs,
Thousands of little boys and girls raising their innocent hands.

text:
When children’s voices ring out on the lawn,  
And soft murmurs move through the hollow,  
The years of my youth return bright in my thoughts,  
And my face grows pale and green.
source_text:
When the voices of children are heard on the green,
And whisperings are in the dale,
The days of my youth rise fresh in my mind,
My face turns green and pale.

text:
Your offer o’ love is but a bawbee,
My tocher’s the price ye’d fainly hae;
But if ye’re sly, I’m doubly wary,
Sae wi’ anither ye may bide your day.
Ye’re like the timber of yon rotted wood,
Ye’re like the rind o’ yon decayed tree,
Ye’ll slide frae me like a thread without a knot,
And break your honour wi’ mair than me.
source_text:
Your proffer o’ luve’s an airle-penny,
My tocher’s the bargain ye wad buy;
But an ye be crafty, I am cunnin’,
Sae ye wi anither your fortune may try.
Ye’re like to the timmer o’ yon rotten wood,
Ye’re like to the bark o’ yon rotten tree,
Ye’ll slip frae me like a knotless thread,
And ye’ll crack your credit wi’ mae nor me.

text:
Who could have known what would return to me?
Who can say I am not savoring this?
Who can say that, despite the miles, I am nearly gazing at you
now, though you cannot see me?
source_text:
Who was to know what should come home to me?
Who knows but I am enjoying this?
Who knows, for all the distance, but I am as good as looking at you
now, for all you cannot see me?

text:
Now summon once more the President’s marshal, bring forth the government cannon,  
call back the thunderers from Congress, and stage another procession,  
with foot soldiers and dragoons to keep watch.
source_text:
Now call for the President’s marshal again, bring out the government cannon,
Fetch home the roarers from Congress, make another procession,
guard it with foot and dragoons.

text:
He kissed the child, then took his hand  
And led him gently along,  
To his mother, who in sorrow’s shade,  
Through the lonely dale had searched for her boy,  
Weeping as she went.
source_text:
He kissed the child, and by the hand led,
And to his mother brought,
Who in sorrow pale, through the lonely dale,
Her little boy weeping sought.

text:
I’m nobody—who are you?
Are you nobody, too?
Then there are two of us—keep it quiet!
They’d drive us away, you know.
source_text:
I'm nobody! Who are you?
Are you nobody, too?
Then there 's a pair of us -- don't tell!
They 'd banish us, you know.

text:
So my mother spoke, and kissed me,
and so I speak to the little English boy.
When I, freed from the dark cloud, and he from the white,
we leap around God’s tent like lambs in joy,
source_text:
Thus did my mother say, and kissed me,
And thus I say to little English boy.
When I from black, and he from white cloud free,
And round the tent of God like lambs we joy,

text:
Ah, not this marble, lifeless and cold:
Far beyond its base and shaft, widening—the circling bands that grow and gather,
embracing all,
You, Washington, belong to the whole world, to every continent—no less
than to America,
To Europe as well, in every corner, the lord’s castle or the laborer’s
cottage,
To the frozen North, the burning South—the African’s, the Arab’s in his tent,
Old Asia too, with her grave, enduring smile, seated among her ruins;
(Does the ancient greet the new hero? It is only the same—the rightful heir,
carried forward always,
That unconquerable heart and arm—signs of the line that has never been
broken,
Courage, vigilance, patience, faith, the same—and even in defeat,
not defeated:)
Wherever a ship sets sail, or a house is raised on earth, by day or by night,
Through crowded streets of cities, indoors or in the open, in factories or
fields,
Now, or yet to come, or long ago—wherever patriotic wills have been or are,
Wherever Freedom, balanced by Tolerance and governed by Law,
Stands or is rising, there is your true monument.
source_text:
Ah, not this marble, dead and cold:
Far from its base and shaft expanding--the round zones circling,
comprehending,
Thou, Washington, art all the world’s, the continents’ entire--not
yours alone, America,
Europe’s as well, in every part, castle of lord or laborer’s cot,
Or frozen North, or sultry South--the African’s--the Arab’s in his tent,
Old Asia’s there with venerable smile, seated amid her ruins;
(Greets the antique the hero new? ’tis but the same--the heir
legitimate, continued ever,
The indomitable heart and arm--proofs of the never-broken line,
Courage, alertness, patience, faith, the same--e’en in defeat
defeated not, the same:)
Wherever sails a ship, or house is built on land, or day or night,
Through teeming cities’ streets, indoors or out, factories or farms,
Now, or to come, or past--where patriot wills existed or exist,
Wherever Freedom, pois’d by Toleration, sway’d by Law,
Stands or is rising thy true monument.

text:
Hark the mavis’ evening song  
Echoing through Clouden’s woods along;  
Then, to fold them, let us go,  
My bonie Dearie.  
Call the ewes, &c.
source_text:
Hark the mavis’ e’ening sang,
Sounding Clouden’s woods amang;
Then a-faulding let us gang,
My bonie Dearie.
Ca’ the yowes, &c.

text:
I met a lady in the meads,  
So lovely, like a fairy’s child;  
Her hair was long, her step was light,  
And in her eyes there burned a wild.
source_text:
I met a Lady in the Meads
Full beautiful, a faery's child,
Her hair was long, her foot was light
And her eyes were wild.

The words of the true poems give you more than poems,
They give you to form for yourself poems, religions, politics, war,
peace, behavior, histories, essays, daily life, and every thing else,
They balance ranks, colors, races, creeds, and the sexes,
They do not seek beauty, they are sought,
Forever touching them or close upon them follows beauty, longing,
fain, love-sick.

text:
Once there was a young pair,  
Brimful of tender care,  
Who met in a glowing garden  
Where the sacred light  
Had only just drawn back the night’s dark curtains.
source_text:
Once a youthful pair,
Filled with softest care,
Met in garden bright
Where the holy light
Had just removed the curtains of the night.

text:
The darkness seemed to shudder, struck  
by an underground thunder at the cry;  
the hollow shore flung back a thousand echoes  
into the night, as though the sea and sky,  
and earth itself, were glad of newborn freedom,  
for in that name they swore. The bolts were drawn;  
and on the deck, with eyes unaccustomed,  
the captives stood and looked, and each one shrank  
as the wavering torch lit up her face.
source_text:
‘The very darkness shook, as with a blast
Of subterranean thunder, at the cry;
The hollow shore its thousand echoes cast
Into the night, as if the sea and sky,
And earth, rejoiced with new-born liberty,
For in that name they swore! Bolts were undrawn,
And on the deck, with unaccustomed eye
The captives gazing stood, and every one
Shrank as the inconstant torch upon her countenance shone.

text:
JUSTINA:
Oh, if it only were
A delusion; yet I fear a deeper harm.
I feel as though from out my wounded breast
My heart were ripped to shards; yes,
Some deadly spell is cast upon my body.
So fierce the charm that, had not God
Guarded my lowly innocence from injury,
I would have gone to meet my grief and shame
With willing steps.—Livia, come, my cloak,
For I must find shelter from these raging storms
Even in the temple of the Most High God,
Where the faithful worship in the hush of secrecy.
source_text:
JUSTINA:
Oh, would it were
Delusion; but I fear some greater ill.
I feel as if out of my bleeding bosom
My heart was torn in fragments; ay,
Some mortal spell is wrought against my frame;
So potent was the charm that, had not God
Shielded my humble innocence from wrong,
I should have sought my sorrow and my shame
With willing steps.—Livia, quick, bring my cloak,
For I must seek refuge from these extremes
Even in the temple of the highest God
Where secretly the faithful worship.

text:
There’s little Tom Dacre, who wept as his head,
That curled like a lamb’s wool, was clipped and stripped bare;
So I told him, “Be still now, Tom, it doesn’t matter at all,
For once your head is bare, the soot can’t stain your white hair.”
source_text:
There’s little Tom Dacre, who cried when his head,
That curled like a lamb’s back, was shaved; so I said,
‘Hush, Tom! never mind it, for, when your head’s bare,
You know that the soot cannot spoil your white hair.’

text:
Each of its names is a sign that sanctifies  
all power—yes, the phantom, the dream, the shadow  
of power—lust, falsehood, hatred, pride, and folly;  
the mold from which all fraud and cruelty are cast,  
a law to which mankind has been delivered;  
and human love, too, is a name as familiar  
as that of a beloved mother, whom the murderer  
laid in a blood-dark grave, and cast into night,  
then gathered her bewildered children close and claimed them as his own.
source_text:
‘“Its names are each a sign which maketh holy
All power—ay, the ghost, the dream, the shade
Of power—lust, falsehood, hate, and pride, and folly;
The pattern whence all fraud and wrong is made,
A law to which mankind has been betrayed;
And human love, is as the name well known
Of a dear mother, whom the murderer laid
In bloody grave, and into darkness thrown,
Gathered her wildered babes around him as his own.

text:
Once a dream cast down a shadow  
Across my bed, kept safe by angels,  
So that an ant went wandering off  
Where I thought, in sleep, I lay upon the grass.
source_text:
Once a dream did weave a shade
O’er my angel-guarded bed,
That an emmet lost its way
Where on grass methought I lay.

text:
So I must puzzle over the hint  
And read the sign askew,  
And stumble often, if at last  
I follow the divine clue.
source_text:
So I must baffle at the hint
And cipher at the sign,
And make much blunder, if at last
I take the clew divine.

text:
And if I win—oh, gun at sea,  
Oh, bells that ring in steeples, please  
At first, sound it slowly!  
For heaven is something else,  
imagined, and then suddenly awake within,  
and might overwhelm me so!
source_text:
And if I gain, -- oh, gun at sea,
Oh, bells that in the steeples be,
At first repeat it slow!
For heaven is a different thing
Conjectured, and waked sudden in,
And might o'erwhelm me so!

text:
Day still rattles on, too,  
and stealth moves slow;  
the sun has gotten only  
to the third sycamore.  
The rooster cries,  
“Who’s there?”  
and echoes, rattling off,  
sneer, “Where?”  
while the old couple, just waking,  
imagine sunrise left the door unlatched!
source_text:
Day rattles, too,
Stealth's slow;
The sun has got as far
As the third sycamore.
Screams chanticleer,
"Who's there?"
And echoes, trains away,
Sneer -- "Where?"
While the old couple, just astir,
Fancy the sunrise left the door ajar!

text:
O Melancholy, avert thine eyes and flee!  
O Music, Music, breathe in sorrow's strain!  
O Echo, Echo, on some farther day,  
From Lethe’s isles, come sighing back again—  
Ye spirits of grief, sing not your “Woe is me!”  
For Isabel, dear Isabel, shall die;  
Shall die a death too lonely, bowed, and vain,  
Now that they have taken from her Basil plain.
source_text:
O Melancholy, turn thine eyes away!
O Music, Music, breathe despondingly!
O Echo, Echo, on some other day,
From isles Lethean, sigh to us--O sigh!
Spirits of grief, sing not your "Well-a-way!"
For Isabel, sweet Isabel, will die;
Will die a death too lone and incomplete,
Now they have ta'en away her Basil sweet.

Belief I sing, and preparation;
As Life and Nature are not great with reference to the present only,
But greater still from what is yet to come,
Out of that formula for thee I sing.

"Tell him it wasn't a practised writer,
You guessed, from the way the sentence toiled;
You could hear the bodice tug, behind you,
As if it held but the might of a child;
You almost pitied it, you, it worked so.
Tell him -- No, you may quibble there,
For it would split his heart to know it,
And then you and I were silenter.

text:
The air is not a fragrance, it carries no trace of  
distillation, it is without scent,  
It is for my mouth forever, I am in love with it,  
I will go to the bank by the wood and stand unhidden, stripped bare,  
I am frantic for it to touch me.
source_text:
The atmosphere is not a perfume, it has no taste of the
distillation, it is odorless,
It is for my mouth forever, I am in love with it,
I will go to the bank by the wood and become undisguised and naked,
I am mad for it to be in contact with me.

text:
“You little child, still wrapped in Jove and May!”
and, catching him: “By this sign I’ll track
my splendid herds, and you will lead the way.”
Then Hermes of Cyllene, from the grass,
as if in truth eager to be gone,
rose, lifting both hands toward his face,
and from his shoulders drew his swaddling cloth up over both ears—“What are you going to do”
source_text:
‘You little swaddled child of Jove and May!
And seized him:—‘By this omen I shall trace
My noble herds, and you shall lead the way.’—
Cyllenian Hermes from the grassy place,
Like one in earnest haste to get away,
Rose, and with hands lifted towards his face
Round both his ears up from his shoulders drew
His swaddling clothes, and—‘What mean you to do

text:
The children were set beneath two guardians’ care,  
And one, Mr. Abbey, shouldering the burden alone,  
At once withdrew John from school, and bound him  
For five years to a surgeon in Edmonton.
source_text:
The children were now put under the care of two guardians, one of whom,
Mr. Abbey, taking the sole responsibility, immediately removed John from
school and apprenticed him for five years to a surgeon at Edmonton.

text:
What could poor Jeanie choose to do?  
She had nae heart to say him nay;  
At last she blushed her sweet assent,  
And love was aye betwixt the twa.
source_text:
Now what could artless Jeanie do?
She had nae will to say him na:
At length she blush’d a sweet consent,
And love was aye between them twa.

text:
Not yours alone, proud truths of the world,  
Not yours alone, O facts of modern science,  
But old myths and fables, Asia’s, Africa’s fables,  
The spirit’s far-flung rays, the dreams set loose,  
The deep-diving Bibles and legends,  
The bold designs of poets, the elder faiths;  
O you temples, fairer than lilies beneath the rising sun!  
O you tales that defy the known, slipping free of the known,  
climbing toward heaven!  
You radiant towers, high and dazzling, pinnacled, red as roses,  
burnished with gold!  
Towers of fable, immortal, shaped from mortal dreams!  
You too I welcome, and hold you wholly as the rest!  
You too I sing with joy.
source_text:
Not you alone proud truths of the world,
Nor you alone ye facts of modern science,
But myths and fables of eld, Asia’s, Africa’s fables,
The far-darting beams of the spirit, the unloos’d dreams,
The deep diving bibles and legends,
The daring plots of the poets, the elder religions;
O you temples fairer than lilies pour’d over by the rising sun!
O you fables spurning the known, eluding the hold of the known,
mounting to heaven!
You lofty and dazzling towers, pinnacled, red as roses, burnish’d
with gold!
Towers of fables immortal fashion’d from mortal dreams!
You too I welcome and fully the same as the rest!
You too with joy I sing.

text:
How dear is the shepherd’s gentle fate!  
From dawn until dusk he wanders on;  
All day long he keeps pace with his flock,  
And his mouth is full of praise and song.
source_text:
How sweet is the shepherd’s sweet lot!
From the morn to the evening he strays;
He shall follow his sheep all the day,
And his tongue shall be fillèd with praise.

text:
Darest thou linger, O Herald? Take heart, ye hounds  
Of Hell: what if the Son of Maia soon  
Should make us both his prey and pastime—who  
Can long delight the Omnipotent?
source_text:
FIRST FURY:
Darest thou delay, O Herald! take cheer, Hounds
Of Hell: what if the Son of Maia soon
Should make us food and sport—who can please long
The Omnipotent?

text:
Peter is a many-faceted Peter, a Peter turned all angles. He shifts  
his colors like a chameleon, his coat like a snake. He is a Proteus  
among Peters. At first he was sublime, pathetic, stirring, profound;  
then merely dull; then plain and dull again; and now dull—oh, so deeply dull! it is  
a thoroughly lawful dullness.
source_text:
Peter is a polyhedric Peter, or a Peter with many sides. He changes
colours like a chameleon, and his coat like a snake. He is a Proteus
of a Peter. He was at first sublime, pathetic, impressive, profound;
then dull; then prosy and dull; and now dull—oh so very dull! it is
an ultra-legitimate dulness.

text:
Nor had I time to love; yet since  
Some labor must be done,  
The small exertion love requires  
Seemed work enough for one.
source_text:
Nor had I time to love; but since
Some industry must be,
The little toil of love, I thought,
Was large enough for me.

text:
What was I then? She lay asleep among the dead.  
Glory and joy and peace had passed away.  
Does the cloud perish when the beams have fled  
That gilded all its edges into day? Or, dark and lonely,  
Does it not move through paths of night unknown,  
With outstretched wings by its own wind upborne,  
To pour rain down upon the earth below? The stars are shown  
When the cold moon sharpens her silver horn,  
And make the wide dark not alone, not forlorn.
source_text:
What then was I? She slumbered with the dead.
Glory and joy and peace, had come and gone.
Doth the cloud perish, when the beams are fled
Which steeped its skirts in gold? or, dark and lone,
Doth it not through the paths of night unknown,
On outspread wings of its own wind upborne
Pour rain upon the earth? The stars are shown,
When the cold moon sharpens her silver horn
Under the sea, and make the wide night not forlorn.

text:
No mercy then for iron or steel;  
The brawny, bonny ploughman’s heel  
Drives hard o’er all with sturdy wheel,  
And forceful stroke,  
Till block and anvil ring and reel  
With din and smoke.
source_text:
Nae mercy then, for airn or steel;
The brawnie, banie, ploughman chiel,
Brings hard owrehip, wi’ sturdy wheel,
The strong forehammer,
Till block an’ studdie ring an reel,
Wi’ dinsome clamour.

text:
The eyes around had drained them dry,
And breaths were gathering, sure,
For that final charge, when the king
Would stand revealed in all his power.
source_text:
The eyes beside had wrung them dry,
And breaths were gathering sure
For that last onset, when the king
Be witnessed in his power.

text:
As Milton, in Lycidas, makes amends  
for bringing in his charge against the Church,  
so Keats must pardon this fierce flame he lends  
against hard dealers, base and false in work,  

a sudden burst of wrath he deems unfit  
for this soft tale of pity, tender and dear.
source_text:
ll. 145-52. As in Lycidas Milton apologizes for the introduction of
his attack on the Church, so Keats apologizes for the introduction of
this outburst of indignation against cruel and dishonourable dealers,
which he feels is unsuited to the tender and pitiful story.

text:
Once more he woke, and through a weary night  
Of love and sorrow, anguish held him fast,  
Had not Isabel's swift eye been set  
On every sign that on his brow was writ;  
She saw him growing pale, as one near dead,  
And straight her cheek was all a sudden flush;  
She lisped, "Lorenzo!"—there her timid search  
Fell still; yet in her face and tone he read the rest.
source_text:
So once more he had wak'd and anguished
A dreary night of love and misery,
If Isabel's quick eye had not been wed
To every symbol on his forehead high;
She saw it waxing very pale and dead,
And straight all flush'd; so, lisped tenderly,
"Lorenzo!"--here she ceas'd her timid quest,
But in her tone and look he read the rest.

text:
The vermeil flowers that open in the light  
Are only frozen, waiting to return;  
So, gentle deceiver, quiet my heart tonight,  
Though all your visions may not be true, but burn,—
source_text:
The vermeil tinted flowers that blossom;
Are frozen but to bud anew,
Then sweet deceiver calm my bosom,
Although thy visions be not true,—

text:
With each dawn their love grew softer, more dear,  
With each dusk it sank more deep and tender still;  
He could not move in house, in field, nor near  
The garden plots, but her whole form would fill  
His every sight; and his unceasing voice  
Was sweeter to her than trees’ faint sound or stream  
That runs concealed; her lute-string, by her choice,  
Gave back his name, and spoiled her half-wrought seam.

“I will not wrong her, by all saints I swear,”  
Quoth Porphyro, “may I ne’er find grace  
When my frail voice shall murmur its last prayer,  
If I so much as stir one silken trace  
Of her soft ringlets, or with brutal face  
Regard her beauty in a ruffian mood:  
Good Angela, trust me by these tears I grace;  
Or else, within a moment’s swiftest flood,  
I’ll wake with dreadful cries my foes, and front them, though they’re more fanged than wolves and brood.”
source_text:
"I will not harm her, by all saints I swear,"
Quoth Porphyro: "O may I ne'er find grace
When my weak voice shall whisper its last prayer,
If one of her soft ringlets I displace,
Or look with ruffian passion in her face:
Good Angela, believe me by these tears;
Or I will, even in a moment's space,
Awake, with horrid shout, my foemen's ears,
And beard them, though they be more fang'd than wolves and
bears."

text:
Lord Gregory, dost thou not recall  
The grove by bonie Irwine’s shore,  
Where first I owned that maiden love  
I long, long had denied before?  
How oft didst thou then pledge and vow  
That thine I should forever be!  
And my fond heart, so true at core,  
Ne’er once mistrusted faith in thee.
source_text:
Lord Gregory, mind’st thou not the grove
By bonie Irwine side,
Where first I own’d that virgin love
I lang, lang had denied.
How aften didst thou pledge and vow
Thou wad for aye be mine!
And my fond heart, itsel’ sae true,
It ne’er mistrusted thine.

text:
The angels, passing by that road,
My dusty heart found sight;
Softly lifted it from labor
And bore it up to God.
There—sandals for the barefoot;
There—snatched from out the gales,
The blue heavens, hand in hand,
Guide all the drifting sails.
source_text:
The angels, happening that way,
This dusty heart espied;
Tenderly took it up from toil
And carried it to God.
There, -- sandals for the barefoot;
There, -- gathered from the gales,
Do the blue havens by the hand
Lead the wandering sails.

text:
By Oughtertyre there springs the oak,  
And on Yarrow’s banks the birken grove;  
But Phemie was a fairer maid  
Than all the braes of Yarrow e’er have proved.  
Merry, merry, &c.
source_text:
By Oughtertyre grows the aik,
On Yarrow banks the birken shaw;
But Phemie was a bonier lass
Than braes o’ Yarrow ever saw.
Blythe, blythe, &c.

text:
CYPRIAN:
For a task so low and secondary,
he could have used the Genii, good or dark—
those spirits so named by scholars,
wandering the world to stir in us to good or ill,
and from whose power, and from whose very being,
we may rightly take our own immortality.
So God could easily, without stooping
in his own person to some crude untruth,
have turned the heart through such an agency
to its rightful end.
source_text:
CYPRIAN:
But for a purpose thus subordinate
He might have employed Genii, good or evil,—
A sort of spirits called so by the learned,
Who roam about inspiring good or evil,
And from whose influence and existence we
May well infer our immortality.
Thus God might easily, without descent
To a gross falsehood in his proper person,
Have moved the affections by this mediation
To the just point.

text:
When my mother died, I was still so young,  
And my father sold me while my tongue  
Could hardly call out, “Weep! weep! weep! weep!”  
So I sweep your chimneys, and in soot I sleep.
source_text:
When my mother died I was very young,
And my father sold me while yet my tongue
Could scarcely cry ‘Weep! weep! weep! weep!’
So your chimneys I sweep, and in soot I sleep.

text:
Shall we wander, my love,
into the dusk-lit grove,
when the moon climbs clear and white;
there I’ll breathe, so low,
what I cannot let show
in the full and open light.
source_text:
Shall we roam, my love,
To the twilight grove,
When the moon is rising bright;
Oh, I’ll whisper there,
In the cool night-air,
What I dare not in broad daylight!

text:
It is the storm-lit beauty of dread;  
for from the serpents flashes a brazen gleam  
stirred by that tangled, inescapable mistake  
that turns the air to a trembling haze,  
a shifting mirror, strange and ever-changing,  
of all the terror and the beauty there—  
a woman’s face, with serpent locks,  
staring in death up at Heaven from those wet rocks.
source_text:
’Tis the tempestuous loveliness of terror;
For from the serpents gleams a brazen glare
Kindled by that inextricable error,
Which makes a thrilling vapour of the air
Become a ... and ever-shifting mirror
Of all the beauty and the terror there—
A woman’s countenance, with serpent-locks,
Gazing in death on Heaven from those wet rocks.

text:
At nineteen, one year before his apprenticeship was done,  
he fell out with his master, left him, and went on  
to London, there to study on at St. Thomas’s Hospital and Guy’s.  
Yet slowly, in the months that followed, although a diligent  
and capable student of medicine, Keats came to see  
that poetry was his truest calling; and, once he was of age,  
despite his guardian’s resistance, he chose to leave the medical art  
and give his whole life to letters.
source_text:
When he was nineteen, a year before his apprenticeship came to an end,
he quarrelled with his master, left him, and continued his training in
London as a student at St. Thomas's Hospital and Guy's. Gradually,
however, during the months that followed, though he was an industrious
and able medical student, Keats came to realize that poetry was his true
vocation; and as soon as he was of age, in spite of the opposition of
his guardian, he decided to abandon the medical profession and devote
his life to literature.

text:
Small is the subject of my song, yet the greatest: the self—
a plain, distinct human being. For the New World, this I sing.
The whole of the body, head to toe, I sing. Not face alone,
nor mind alone, is fit for the Muse; the full shape,
I say, is far more worthy. I sing the female as equal to the male.
And I do not stop with the self. I speak the modern word,
the word En-Masse.
My days I sing, and the lands—between them, the cruel gap of war.
(O friend, whoever you are, at last arriving here to begin,
I feel in every leaf the weight of your hand, and I return it.
So on our journey, setting foot on the road, and more than once,
let us go on, bound together.)
source_text:
Small the theme of my Chant, yet the greatest--namely, One’s-Self--
a simple, separate person. That, for the use of the New World, I sing.
Man’s physiology complete, from top to toe, I sing. Not physiognomy alone,
nor brain alone, is worthy for the Muse;--I say the Form complete
is worthier far. The Female equally with the Male, I sing.
Nor cease at the theme of One’s-Self. I speak the word of the
modern, the word En-Masse.
My Days I sing, and the Lands--with interstice I knew of hapless War.
(O friend, whoe’er you are, at last arriving hither to commence, I
feel through every leaf the pressure of your hand, which I return.
And thus upon our journey, footing the road, and more than once, and
link’d together let us go.)

text:
With her two brothers dwelt this comely dame,  
Enriched by traffic from the elder line,  
And for their gain did many a weary hand  
Grow gaunt in firelit mines and clanging mills,  
And many a once proud, sinew-straining loins  
Were melted into blood beneath the lash;—  
And many, with hollow eyes, stood all day long  
In the bright river, gleaning golden drift.
